topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara sat hunched over the worn oak desk, the crisp, glossy paper feeling alien beneath her fingertips. It was her essay, meticulously crafted over months, suddenly displayed on a university admissions portal as a model submission. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, spreading outward until her hands began to tremble, causing the papers to flutter slightly. She reread the carefully chosen metaphors, the nuanced reflections on her experiences, and a sharp, hollow ache settled behind her ribs. She pushed back from the desk, the sound of the chair scraping the floor echoing too loudly in the quiet room. Her breath hitched, shallow and quick, as she stared at the screen, unable to reconcile the private vulnerability of her writing with its public, impersonal presentation.

[story 2]

Mark felt a sudden, sickening lurch in his chest when he saw it. His application essay, the piece of his soul he’d poured into those words, was displayed prominently under "Featured Examples." He blinked hard, trying to force his vision to recalibrate, but the words swam, blurring into an overwhelming wash of panic. He stood up abruptly, knocking his chair backward with a jarring clatter. His palms grew slick with sweat, and he started pacing the small room, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. "No," he whispered, the sound barely audible, thick with disbelief. He ran a hand over his face, feeling the heat rising in his cheeks, a desperate urge to disappear.

[story 3]

When Chloe finally found the document tucked away in the shared drive, a wave of nausea washed over her. It was her personal narrative, the one she’d agonized over for weeks, now sitting there, polished and ready for consumption by thousands of future scholars. Her jaw clenched so tightly her molars ached. She sank onto the floor, pulling her knees to her chest, wrapping her arms around herself as if trying to contain an internal tremor. Her gaze kept flicking back to the screen, a frantic, darting motion, as if the words themselves held some dangerous, unforeseen consequence. She didn't move for a long time, just breathing in short, ragged gasps.
